# Understanding Targeted Ads in Digital Marketing

![Targeted ads explained in digital marketing.](https://www.institutedata.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/Targeted-Ads-Explained-in-Digital-Marketing.png)

#### Stay Informed With Our Weekly Newsletter

Receive crucial updates on the ever-evolving landscape of technology and innovation.

By clicking 'Sign Up', I acknowledge that my information will be used in accordance with the Institute of Data's [Privacy Policy](/legal/privacy/).

Targeted ads in digital marketing have revolutionized the way businesses connect with their audiences.

Studies show a [5.3x increase in click-through rates](https://webtribunal.net/blog/targeted-advertising-statistics#gref) (CTR) when targeted ads are used, and around 37% of social media users make purchases from these ads.

Surprisingly, despite these advantages, 76% of marketers underutilize targeting options, revealing missed opportunities for enhancing the effectiveness of their marketing efforts.

This article delves into the fascinating world of targeted ads, explaining how they work, their benefits, and how they’re shaping the future of marketing.

## The essence of targeted ads in digital marketing

![Digital marketers planning for targeted ads in digital marketing.](https://www.institutedata.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/The-essence-of-targeted-ads-in-digital-marketing.png)

![Digital marketers planning for targeted ads in digital marketing.](https://www.institutedata.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/The-essence-of-targeted-ads-in-digital-marketing.png)

In digital marketing, targeted ads are a form of online advertising in which advertisements are selected and served to consumers based on their previous browsing behaviour, demographic information, and other personal data.

This personalized approach ensures the right message reaches the right people at the right time.

Targeted ads in digital marketing are not a new concept, but their recent application has taken them to new heights.

With the advent of sophisticated algorithms and data analytics, targeted ads have become more precise, effective, and efficient than ever before.

### How targeted ads work

Targeted digital marketing ads operate on the data collection and analysis principle.

Your activities, preferences, and behavior are tracked and stored when you browse the internet.

This data is then analyzed to create a profile that helps advertisers understand your interests and needs.

Once your profile is created, advertisers use it to serve ads that align with your interests.

For instance, if you’ve been searching for running shoes, you might start seeing ads for sports equipment. This is targeted advertising in action.

### The role of cookies in targeted ads

Cookies play a crucial role in targeted ads.

These small pieces of data are stored on your device when you visit a website.

They track your online behavior, remember your preferences, and provide valuable data for targeted ads.

However, it’s important to note that cookies are not the only tool used to collect data in targeted ads.

Other methods include pixel tags, [web beacons](https://iapp.org/resources/article/web-beacon/), and mobile device identifiers, each offering unique capabilities and benefits.

## The benefits of targeted ads in digital marketing

![Business with benefits using targeted ads in digital marketing strategy.](https://www.institutedata.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/The-benefits-of-targeted-ads-in-digital-marketing.png)

![Business with benefits using targeted ads in digital marketing strategy.](https://www.institutedata.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/The-benefits-of-targeted-ads-in-digital-marketing.png)

Targeted ads in digital marketing offer numerous benefits to both businesses and consumers.

Businesses can use them as a cost-effective way to reach potential customers, increase brand awareness, and drive sales.

They offer consumers a personalized browsing experience, showing relevant and interesting ads, which can lead to higher conversion rates.

### Improved return on investment

One of the key benefits of targeted ads is the improved return on investment (ROI).

By focusing on a specific audience, businesses can reduce wasted ad spend and increase the effectiveness of their marketing campaigns.

Moreover, targeted ads allow businesses to measure their performance more accurately.

With detailed analytics, businesses can track the success of their ads, make data-driven decisions, and continually optimize their campaigns for better results.

### Enhanced customer engagement

Targeted ads also enhance customer engagement.

By delivering relevant and personalized content, businesses can capture their audience’s attention, encourage interaction, and build stronger relationships.

Furthermore, targeted ads can help businesses understand their customers better.

By analyzing the data collected, businesses can gain insights into their customers’ behavior, preferences, and needs, allowing them to refine their marketing strategies and deliver a better customer experience.

## The future of targeted ads in digital marketing

![Marketing team using AI and ML for targeted ads in digital marketing.](https://www.institutedata.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/The-future-of-targeted-ads-in-digital-marketing.png)

![Marketing team using AI and ML for targeted ads in digital marketing.](https://www.institutedata.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/The-future-of-targeted-ads-in-digital-marketing.png)

As technology continues to evolve, so will targeted ads in digital marketing.

With advancements in artificial intelligence, machine learning, and data analytics, targeted ads are set to become even more precise and personalized.

Moreover, as consumers become more aware of their digital footprint, there will be a greater emphasis on privacy and data protection.

This will undoubtedly shape the future of targeted ads, with businesses needing to balance personalization with privacy.

### Increased use of AI and machine learning

Artificial intelligence (AI) and [machine learning](https://www.institutedata.com/us/blog/mastering-machine-learning-unlocking-the-potential-of-advanced-algorithms-for-enhanced-performance/) are set to play a significant role in the future of targeted ads.

These technologies can analyze vast amounts of data quickly and accurately, enabling more precise targeting and personalization.

Furthermore, [AI and machine learning](https://www.institutedata.com/us/blog/exploring-the-applications-of-mathematics-and-statistics-in-machine-learning-and-ai-2/) can predict consumer behavior, allowing businesses to anticipate customer needs and deliver timely and relevant ads.

This predictive capability will be a game-changer in targeted advertising.

### Focus on privacy and data protection

Privacy and data protection will be a key focus in the future of targeted ads in digital marketing.

With increasing concerns about data privacy, businesses must be transparent about how they collect, use, and store consumer data.

Moreover, US businesses must comply with data protection regulations, such as the [General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) in Europe](https://www.itgovernanceusa.com/data-protection-and-eu-data-protection-regulation).

This will require a careful approach to data collection and targeted advertising.

## Conclusion

Targeted ads are integral to digital marketing, offering numerous benefits to businesses and consumers alike.

As technology continues to evolve, targeted ads will become even more precise, personalized, and effective, shaping the future of digital marketing.

SUMMARY:
As advertisers' targeted-ad algorithms improve, one might expect that their laser-focus would become even more fine-tuned and profitable.

FULL CONTENT:
![Yale School of Management logo](/themes/custom/som_2025/ui/dist/images/yalesom_logo_stacked_dark-min.svg)
![Yale School of Management logo](/themes/custom/som_2025/ui/dist/images/yalesom_logo_shield-min.svg)

### [Yale Center for Customer Insights](/centers/center-for-customer-insights) Navigation Menu

![5 blocks, with one block in the middle showing a green target on it ](/sites/default/files/styles/scaled_140_/public/2023-06/Blog-Picture-sm_1.jpg.webp?itok=novmHrJL)

# As the Accuracy of Targeted Advertising Increases, So Do the Pitfalls

New research from Yale SOM professor Jiwoong Shin highlights how the predictive power of targeted advertising can be undercut by ignoring the misaligned incentives of advertisers and consumers.

Marketers understand by now that when it comes to shelling out for ads, it pays to target the consumers most likely to buy their products. [Study](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/abs/10.1287/mksc.1050.0117) after [study](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/TargetedAdvertising_34ea7eb7-2c93-4999-add9-406c9f01f39c.pdf) has established that the effectiveness of advertising increases when it is strategically directed—something that advertisers can do with increasing precision thanks to online data collection.

As advertisers’ targeted-ad algorithms improve, one might expect that their laser-focus would become even more fine-tuned and profitable.  But while targeted-ad algorithms have improved, the regulatory environment and the increasingly present reality of the so-called “cookieless future” has created doubts for how targeting can be utilized to increase profitability. Yale SOM professor Jiwoong Shin, echoes these doubts. “This [fine-tuned targeting and increasing profitability] is the core promise for the bright future of the digital advertising industry,” he writes in a new paper co-authored by Z. Eddie Ning, assistant professor of marketing at the University of British Columbia, and Jungju Yu, assistant professor of marketing at the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology. “However, the reality is not necessarily playing out so well.”

In their paper, the researchers explore the ways in which targeted advertising can backfire. Since the arrival of regulations (like the EU’s [GDPR](https://gdpr.eu/what-is-gdpr/) and California’s [CCPA](https://oag.ca.gov/privacy/ccpa)) that allow consumers to limit the tracking of their online lives, advertisers can more easily undermine their own efforts to maximize profits—by unwittingly pushing the consumers they’re targeting to opt-out of data collection.

To study how this might happen, the researchers built a model to simulate online interactions among a firm advertising a product and a group of potential consumers.

One of their operating premises was that many people actually interpret targeted ads as personalized recommendations, a tendency that [previous research](https://beesystrategy.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/02/Chris-Summers_An-Audience-of-One.pdf) has established. Advertisers are, of course, aware of this tendency. Given that their own motivation is maximizing sales (and not necessarily offering thoughtful recommendations for their own sake), the researchers conjectured that **advertisers would be incentivized to take advantage of consumers’ trust in the personal match of products featured in their targeted ads**.

This is exactly what their model revealed. “Given the misaligned incentives, the firm’s better prediction capability does not necessarily translate into more accurate targeting,” the researchers write.

The model showed that advertisers were likely to take advantage of consumers’ trust in the accuracy of targeted-ad prediction by “cheating” and broadening the ad’s viewership, sending it to people who were assumed to be weak matches with the advertised product. The advertiser was simply betting that even some of these weaker matches may buy the product, an outcome made more likely by their seeing the ad as a recommendation.

At least, this was what happened when the researchers modeled a scenario in which product prices were kept fixed. Next, they modeled a slightly different scenario: one in which product prices could fluctuate depending on the firm’s confidence in the accuracy of its predictions. In this case, the advertiser was less likely to send ads to weak-match customer and instead simply increased prices for best-match customers.

Unlike the scenario with fixed product prices, this one was likely to backfire for advertisers. As consumers confronted higher prices, they were driven to protect their privacy and disable ad targeting. As a result, advertisers were left empty-handed, without the option to target those potential consumers at all.

“These results illustrate the limit of prediction technology in reducing market friction,” the co-authors write. Advertisers would do well to be aware of the misaligned incentives dictating these online interactions around ad-targeting, clicking, buying, and opting-out. “As privacy laws go into effect, consumers’ potential decision to opt-out may discipline the advertiser’s incentives, ultimately influencing the advertiser’s targeting strategy and consumer welfare.” The resounding result of the research: advertisers should err on the side of caution with the use of re-targeted ads if they want to maximize profits.

Introduction to AI in Audience Targeting
----------------------------------------

[Audience targeting](https://adcopy.ai/post/how-to-create-retargeting-audience-on-facebook) has been revolutionized by artificial intelligence (AI), enabling marketers to navigate the vast digital landscape with precision and efficiency. By leveraging AI, advertisers can analyze large datasets to identify key audience segments, predict consumer behavior, and enhance personalization. AI-driven tools process user data through sophisticated algorithms, discerning patterns and preferences that humans might overlook. This intelligence leads to more effective ad placements, optimizing the return on investment by delivering the right message to the right audience at the right time. With AI, audience targeting transcends traditional demographics, tapping into dynamic insights that fuel engagement and drive marketing success.

Understanding the Role of AI in Modern Advertising
--------------------------------------------------

Artificial Intelligence (AI) revolutionizes modern advertising by enabling precise audience targeting and personalized content delivery. AI systems analyze vast data sets to identify patterns and predict consumer behavior. This enables marketers to:

*   Segment audiences with unprecedented accuracy

*   Forecast purchasing trends

*   Tailor advertisements to individual preferences

AI-driven tools also automate ad bidding and placement, optimizing marketing budgets and increasing campaign ROI. By harnessing AI’s computational power, advertisers can engage consumers more effectively, enhancing both the user experience and brand loyalty.

The Emergence of AI-Powered Programmatic Advertising Platforms
--------------------------------------------------------------

AI-powered programmatic advertising platforms represent a paradigm shift in how digital campaigns are executed. Through machine learning algorithms, these platforms analyze vast sets of data, enabling highly efficient audience targeting. Advertisers can now automate the decision-making process with unparalleled precision.

*   Real-time bidding is revolutionized, with AI evaluating the best ad placement opportunities within milliseconds.

*   Predictive analytics inform on potential consumer behavior, allowing for proactive campaign adjustments.

*   Natural language processing and image recognition capabilities lead to enhanced contextual and visual targeting.

*   Performance metrics are continuously optimized, as AI platforms learn from each ad interaction, adjusting strategies on the fly.

These platforms are transforming digital advertising into a more dynamic, responsive, and effective ecosystem.

Leveraging AI for Precise Demicrobial Targeting
-----------------------------------------------

Artificial Intelligence is revolutionizing how marketers connect with specific demographics. By analyzing vast amounts of data, AI algorithms can identify intricate patterns and predict consumer behavior with unprecedented accuracy. This allows for:

*   Micro-targeting campaigns to distinct demographic groups

*   Personalizing messages that resonate deeply with audience subsets

*   Streamlining media buying to focus on channels favored by target demographics

*   Adjusting marketing strategies in real-time based on demographic engagement feedback

Marketers harnessing AI’s power are achieving higher engagement rates and ROI by delivering content that aligns with the nuanced preferences of their intended audiences.

Behavioral and Predictive Analytics in Audience Segmentation
------------------------------------------------------------

Behavioral and Predictive Analytics have revolutionized audience segmentation in advertising. With these tools, advertisers can:

*   Analyze historical data to understand consumer behavior patterns.

*   Predict future actions of consumers based on their past interactions.

*   Segment audiences more accurately by likelihood of interest or purchase.

*   Tailor marketing messages to resonate with each specific segment.

*   Enhance the customer experience by anticipating needs and preferences.

These analytics enable a proactive approach, allowing for dynamic targeting that adapts to changing behaviors, ensuring more precise audience targeting and improved campaign performance.

Real-Time Bidding and AI: A Game Changer for Ad Efficiency
----------------------------------------------------------

Real-time bidding (RTB), powered by AI, revolutionizes how ads reach audiences. RTB facilitates instant auction-style bidding for ad impressions, allowing advertisers to compete for ad space in milliseconds as a user loads a webpage. AI enhances this process by:

*   Analyzing vast datasets to predict the outcome of bids and the potential value of impressions.

*   Using predictive analytics to determine the optimal bid for each ad impression, ensuring cost-efficiency.

*   Continuously learning from real-time bidding outcomes to refine bidding strategies and improve return on investment (ROI).

*   Personalizing ad placement by matching user profiles with advertiser targets in real time, increasing the likelihood of engagement.

AI-driven RTB shifts ad placement from a shotgun approach to a targeted sniper tactic, significantly boosting ad efficiency.

Machine Learning and Natural Language Processing in Ad Personalization
----------------------------------------------------------------------

By harnessing [machine learning (ML)](https://adcopy.ai/post/unlocking-the-potential-of-ai) and natural language processing (NLP), advertisers are now able to sift through massive datasets to identify patterns and preferences specific to individual consumers. These AI-driven techniques enable:

*   The analysis of consumer language in reviews and social media posts to tailor ad messaging.

*   Real-time adaptation of ad content to the language and style that resonates with target audiences.

*   Enhanced targeting by predicting user behavior based on previous interactions and expressed sentiments.

*   More relevant ad experiences through personalized recommendations and search results.

Through ML and NLP, ad personalization transcends demographics, reaching the core of individual consumer intent and interest.

The Impact of AI on Ad Spend Optimization and ROI
-------------------------------------------------

Artificial intelligence revolutionizes advertising by enhancing the precision of audience targeting. AI algorithms analyze vast amounts of data, recognizing patterns that humans may overlook. This allows advertisers to allocate their budgets more effectively, directing funds toward channels and demographics that yield higher engagement and conversions. As ad spending is optimized, businesses witness a significant increase in return on investment (ROI), with AI-powered campaigns delivering tailored messaging that resonates with the audience, leading to greater campaign efficiency and profitability.

Case Studies: Brands that Transformed Their Targeting with AI Tools
-------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Netflix**: Leveraged predictive analytics to tailor recommendations, increasing viewer engagement and retention.

*   **Stitch Fix**: Utilized machine learning to personalize fashion selections, significantly improving customer satisfaction and loyalty.

*   **Spotify**: Deployed AI to analyze listening habits, creating tailored playlists that enhanced user experience and time spent on the platform.

*   **Toyota**: Implemented AI-driven tools for precise customer segmentation, leading to more targeted and effective advertising campaigns.

*   **Sephora**: Used AI to offer virtual product try-ons, driving online sales by providing a tailored and interactive shopping experience.

*   **Airbnb**: Applied machine learning algorithms to optimize search results and property recommendations, boosting booking rates.

Ethical Considerations and Privacy Concerns in AI-Driven Targeting
------------------------------------------------------------------

In leveraging AI for audience targeting, advertisers must navigate ethical considerations and privacy concerns responsibly. The potential for AI algorithms to perpetuate biases and infringe on individual privacy is a significant issue. Advertisers should:

*   Ensure transparency in data usage, providing clear consent mechanisms for users.

*   Implement fair data practices to prevent discrimination, maintaining a commitment to ethical AI.

*   Guard against intrusive surveillance, respecting personal boundaries and data protection laws.

*   Apply anonymization techniques to protect user identities from being compromised.

By addressing these concerns, companies can foster trust and maintain ethical standards in AI-driven advertising strategies.

The Future of Advertising with AI: Trends and Predictions
---------------------------------------------------------

As the advertising industry embraces AI, we foresee several key trends:

*   **_Hyper-personalization_**: AI algorithms will refine audience targeting, delivering highly personalized ads based on real-time data and predictive behavior analytics.

*   **_Programmatic Ad Buying_**: AI’s efficiency in programmatic advertising will continue to grow, ensuring cost-effective and precise ad placements.

*   **_Voice and Visual Search_**: Advancements in voice recognition and image analysis will lead to more intuitive and context-aware ads.

*   **_Ethical Targeting_**: Increased scrutiny on data privacy will drive the development of AI that respects consumer boundaries while still providing value.

*   **_Cross-Channel Optimization_**: AI will optimize ad campaigns across multiple platforms and devices, maintaining cohesive messaging and maximizing reach.

These trends underscore AI’s pivotal role in shaping future advertising strategies.

Conclusion: How AI Tools Are Shaping the Landscape of Audience Targeting
------------------------------------------------------------------------

AI tools have revolutionized audience targeting by providing unprecedented precision and efficiency. They enable marketers to analyze large data sets quickly, identifying patterns and predicting consumer behavior with remarkable accuracy. [Machine learning algorithms](https://adcopy.ai/post/using-machine-learning-to-acquire-new-customers-on-social-media) continuously refine targeting strategies, enhancing personalization and relevance. This has resulted in more targeted campaigns, higher conversion rates, and improved ROI. As these tools evolve, they set new standards for how businesses engage with their audience, ensuring that each marketing message is as effective as possible.

SUMMARY:
In order to clarify the relationship between the transparency of the algorithm recommendation and the audience's coping behavior, based on the persuasion theory, this study discusses the mechanism of the influence of the characteristics of the algorithm recommendation information flow on the audience's coping behavior of targeted advertising from the perspective of the flow mode and transmission principle of information. The influence of recommendation algorithm's information flow on targeted advertising audience's coping behavior
===============================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Lijun Chen a b, Rui Sun a, Yuan Yuan a, Xuemei Zhan b

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2024.104168](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2024.104168 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0001691824000453&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Abstract
--------

Targeted advertising equipped with a [recommendation algorithm](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/recommendation-algorithm "Learn more about recommendation algorithm from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can achieve accurate matching between users and recommended content, but overly precise recommendations may exacerbate negative audience reactions or behaviors. Improving the transparency of algorithm recommendation is one of the ways to address audience concerns or skepticism, and transparency guarantees the audience's right to know and thus brings more trust, which will reduce the audience's negative behavior. But increased transparency may also make the audience feel pressured or threatened, and requiring more cognitive and behavioral effort, which was called coping behavior. In order to clarify the relationship between the transparency of the algorithm recommendation and the audience's coping behavior, based on the persuasion theory, this study discusses the mechanism of the influence of the characteristics of the algorithm recommendation information flow on the audience's coping behavior of targeted advertising from the perspective of the flow mode and transmission principle of information. Based on the data of 120 online pretests and 297 formal tests, the results show that the perceived trust and perceived threat caused by the information [flow characteristics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/flow-characteristics "Learn more about flow characteristics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the algorithm recommendation jointly determine the possible coping behaviors of targeted advertising audiences. Additionally, users' self-efficacy regulates the relationship between mental process and coping behavior. Different from previous studies on audience coping behaviors of targeted ads, which mainly start from the perspective of participants and advertising content, this research tries to start from the perspective of information flow. The [research results](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/research-results "Learn more about research results from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") demystify the relationship between recommendation algorithm information flow and the audience's coping behavior, and enrich the algorithmic persuasion framework. The research results have [reference value](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/reference-value "Learn more about reference value from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for the improvement of personalized recommendation effect, and provide a new way to further study the transparency of algorithm recommendation in the field of consumer behavior. Meanwhile, it also provides suggestions for the practices of platforms and advertisers in practice.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000210)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000416)

Keywords
--------

Algorithm recommendation

Information flow

Targeted advertising

Coping behavior

Self-efficacy

1. Introduction
---------------

Targeted advertising is also known as precision advertising or personalized advertising. Its essence is to use data analysis and [data mining technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/data-mining-technique "Learn more about data mining technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to find valuable information and rules from massive online consumer data. Targeted advertising can predict consumers' demands and preferences, and to provide consumers with advertising information that meets their respective needs ([Kai et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0085)). For users, targeted recommendations can lead to products and services more aligned with personal preferences ([Rust, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0180)). With the continuous development of algorithm technology, targeted advertising recommendation for specific audiences on various platforms is becoming more accurate. Algorithm-driven targeted advertising can significantly improve user stickiness and increase corporate revenue ([Miyan et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0140)). User data is the driving force behind the algorithm, and the first step of any effective targeted advertising recommendation requires collecting customer data ([Tucker, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0230)). Accurately targeted advertising is built based on enough customer data; the more the user data is mastered, the more accurate the recommendation is.

In practice, although the fusion of a large amount of users' personal information can improve the prediction accuracy of the [recommendation algorithm](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/recommendation-algorithm "Learn more about recommendation algorithm from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), it often causes users to worry about privacy and security ([Honglei et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0065)). At the same time, too precise recommendation makes consumers feel that the algorithm knows them better than themselves” and the algorithm is controlling them ([Rui et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0175)), which will be manifested in resistance to and avoidance of various recommendation [advertisements](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/advertising "Learn more about advertisements from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Studies show that 74% of Americans limit their online activities due to concerns about their data, and 51% choose not to click on personalized ads ([Strycharz et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0225)). The perception of privacy risk will lead users to improve their privacy concerns and privacy protection intentions, thus leading to the occurrence of advertising [avoidance behaviors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/avoidance-behavior "Learn more about avoidance behaviors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and ultimately affecting the recommendation effect ([Man & Xiuyan, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0135)). Some users even commit to “false interaction,” deliberately [liking](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/liking "Learn more about liking from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") the content they dislike or ignoring the content of interest in response to the algorithm recommendation ([Miyan et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0140)). The increase in users' [negative emotions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/negative-emotion "Learn more about negative emotions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and related coping behaviors will reduce the effectiveness of recommendations. Coping behavior refers to the individual's behavioral efforts to deal with various psychological stress. Therefore, exploring audiences' coping behaviors with targeted advertisements is of important theoretical and [practical significance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/practical-significance "Learn more about practical significance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Currently, the existing studies and explanations of the audience's coping behavior of targeted advertising are mainly carried out from the audience's perspective. Relevant research mainly focuses on two aspects. Some studies are interested in the causes of the coping behavior. These studies follow the technology threat avoidance theory (TTAT) and privacy calculus theory. Technology threat avoidance theory (TTAT) explains how consumers decide to take different coping behaviors through assessment when facing technology threats. Technology threat avoidance theory (TTAT) explains how consumers decide to adopt different coping behaviors through evaluation in the face of technology threats. Privacy calculus theory explains how consumers perceive, evaluate and weigh risks and benefits in the face of privacy risks that may be brought by recommendation algorithms, and generate different coping behaviors. Some studies have also introduced the social contract theory, arguing that when people worry that the social contract between them and the platform is less reliable, they may decide to adopt corresponding coping behaviors to protect their privacy ([Kruikemeier et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0100)). At the same time, it also focuses on the causes of different types of coping behaviors. Meanwhile, some studies discuss how to avoid or weaken the negative behaviors of the audience to improve the recommendation effect of targeted ads. For example, some scholars believe that enhancing the right to know will increase the user's trust and sense of control, thus bringing more acceptance behaviors. For example, Rui and Yingyu believe that advertising transparency significantly affects the actual acceptance behaviors of consumers, and this effect is influenced by the level of privacy concern ([Rui & Yingyu, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0170)).

In essence, targeted advertising is a process of persuading the audience through a platform, and the acceptance or avoidance of consumers is the effect of persuasion. According to the theory of communication persuasion, the process of communication persuasion is affected by participants, information attributes, and flow mode (transmission principle), while existing studies are mostly discussed from the perspective of participants and recommended content, focuses on explaining the mechanism of the audience's coping behavior of targeted advertising, and pays more attention to the result ([Jiawei et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0070); [Yuan & Han, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0245)). There are also studies that pay attention to the problem of algorithm source, and believe that the credibility of algorithm source has a significant impact on user attitude, and users' positive reaction to the algorithm is based on users' perceived credibility of algorithm source ([Ki & Shin, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0090)). However, discussion on the flow mode and transmission principle of information is rare. Therefore, in order to explore the mechanism relationship between the characteristics of algorithm recommendation information flow and the coping behavior of targeted advertising audiences, based on the perspective of communication and persuasion theory, this study examines the different coping behaviors of targeted advertising audiences in different recommendation algorithm information flow scenarios, and examines the mediating role and the moderating role. The research aims to clarify the relationship between the transparency of algorithm recommendation and the coping behavior of the audience, and can provide some suggestions for improving the effectiveness of targeted advertising in practice.

2. Research model and hypotheses
--------------------------------

### 2.1. Background

Precisely targeted advertising relies on massive amounts of personal information, so the protection and reasonable use of personal information has become the focus of attention. The public, society, and government demand explanations for why algorithms make decisions in particular situations, and interpretable algorithms are recognized as an alternative solution to some transparency problems so that users can understand the [internal processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internal-process "Learn more about internal processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of algorithmic models ([Shin, 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0210)). Therefore, the context of obtaining personal information, that is, which information is obtained and how it is obtained, becomes an important consideration to ensure the audience's right to know. The right to know here refers to the audience's right to know how their personal information is obtained and how it is used. In reality, the audience's right to know is often presented in the form of privacy clauses or policies, which generally inform visitors and users of their respective information flow practices in advance and provide them with the choice of participation or withdrawal. However, evidence shows that, in reality, people do not read privacy policies or do not understand them when they read them and cannot read them even if they want to ([Reidenberg & Cranor, 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0160)). As a result, privacy policies do not really address the issue of the right to know, which can lead to different reactions when users are confronted with targeted ads pushed by algorithms based on personal information. The difference in responses reflects users' attitudes towards algorithmic recommendations and the use of personal information.

### 2.2. Recommendation algorithm information flow characteristics and audience coping behavior

From the perspective of algorithm recommendation, the core of targeted advertising is the massive [personal data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/personal-data "Learn more about personal data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") information mastered by the platform. The more information they have, the more accurate the recommendations are. The platform realizes the matching of recommendation and users by [training algorithm](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/training-algorithm "Learn more about training algorithm from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") model ([Chun-L & Cheng, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0040)). As user data continues to be input, algorithms can make more accurate and personalized predictions, and the extent to which users approve of the use of their data determines the operation and performance of algorithm recommendations ([Shin, 2023b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0200)). In practice, the same recommendation may have different effects in different scenarios, the essential reason lies in the data information and the logic characteristics it relies on, that is, the information [flow characteristics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/flow-characteristics "Learn more about flow characteristics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IFC) in algorithm recommendation. The information flow characteristics reflect the platform's attitude and practice towards personal information.

How should the platform process and apply information related to individuals? Most people subconsciously believe it must follow certain norms; if the information flow characteristics conform to the corresponding norms, it will be considered acceptable; otherwise, it will not be acceptable. Therefore, even if the audience's right to know is ensured through privacy policies and other means, people will still adopt different coping behaviors when facing targeted [advertisements](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/advertising "Learn more about advertisements from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Ultimately, whether a platform follows the rules for accessing personal information makes a difference. The key parameters of an information specification are actors (subject, sender, receiver), attributes (type of information), and transmission principles (constraints on information flow). In general, all is well when the flow of information follows entrenched norms; however, violations of these norms often lead to protests and complaints ([Nissenbaum, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0150)). For example, it is considered acceptable to disclose some [personal health information](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/personal-health-information "Learn more about personal health information from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on medical websites and even share it with relevant experts to get relevant medical help. However, suppose users find that personal health information is shared with some shopping websites for the targeted advertising of [related products](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/related-product "Learn more about related products from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In that case, they believe that the information standard has been damaged, and the acceptance degree of qualitative advertising will be greatly affected.

Advertising is based on the assumption that consumer behavior can be modified through cognitive influences ([Lee & Shin, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0110)). In essence, targeted advertising is a process of communication and persuasion; that is, the push process of targeted advertising is information transmission. Its success depends on whether the audience accepts or is persuaded when receiving the recommended advertisement. American psychologist Hovland et al. created the theory of communication and persuasion, believing that the source, content, channel, and information recipient will jointly affect the audience's attitude towards information ([Riley et al., 1954](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0165)). Persuasion communication theory applies not only to traditional information communication situations but also to explain information communication behaviors in the network environment ([Song et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0215)). When it comes to explaining coping behaviors, existing research predominantly adopts the perspective of the audience, who are the recipients of information transmission. However, there is limited research that focuses on the characteristics of [information sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/information-source "Learn more about information sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and their channels. The algorithmic persuasion framework considers that the input of information and data in online communication is the key element in the process of algorithmic persuasion ([Zarouali et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0255)). The information content on which recommendations are generated and the channels through which they are obtained have an important impact on the audience. When audiences know how information is collected and analyzed, they are more likely to accept recommendations, and when this mechanism is transparent enough, audiences are even willing to increase their [information input](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/input-information "Learn more about information input from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to increase recommendations ([Shin, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0195)). In the current highly informationized environment, the recipient, the type of information and the constraint conditions of information flow are constantly changing. The type and carrier of information are more diversified, the flow and transmission of information are more uncontrollable, and the privacy boundary is becoming more blurred. Information technology and its superior ability to collect, analyze and distribute personal information gradually pose a threat to personal privacy. New technology has significantly altered the flow of personal information, earlier conceptions of privacy are no longer up to snuff ([Brandimarte et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0025); [Zhong, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0260)). .In such a digital environment, Privacy is not exclusively, either the right to control personal information or the right to prevent access to a core of such information. Instead, it is about ensuring that personal information flows appropriately, and this varies contextually ([Nissenbaum, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0155)). However, no matter what kind of context, whether online or offline, they are all restricted by information norms. In almost everything we do, events and transactions occur in a context, not only in place but also in politics, customs, and cultural expectations ([Nissenbaum, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0145)). Whether the flow norm of information can be accepted or not depends on whether it is consistent with the current context. Nissenbaum proposed two types of information norms: appropriateness norms and flow or distribution norms. When both types of norms are maintained, the integrity of context is maintained, and when either norm is violated, the integrity of context is violated ([Nissenbaum, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0145)). That is Nissenbaum's conception of privacy “contextual integrity”. The generation of targeted advertising comes from a large amount of individual information, and these [information sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/information-source "Learn more about information sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") will have different impacts on the audience because of the standard degree of flow or distribution. Therefore, the audience's choice of behavior strategies for targeted advertising depends on whether the characteristics of information flow which generate the advertising can be accepted, that is, whether the personal information relied on by the targeted advertising recommendation algorithm is suitable or not for a specific context and whether the distribution or information flow respects the corresponding contextual norms.

Based on the current technical principles and the contextual integrity principle, the information flow characteristics of recommendation algorithms are summarized into the following two categories: (1) Acceptable information flow characteristics, that is, conforming to the norms of appropriate content and appropriate flow. (2) Unacceptable information flow characteristics, that is, they do not conform to appropriate content and appropriate flow specifications. Appropriate content refers to the information content that the audience is willing to share with the platform, such as not involving sensitive personal information. Appropriate flow means that the flow of information is acceptable to the audience, such as there is no abuse of personal information across different platforms. If the audience of targeted advertising perceives inappropriateness information such as discrimination in the advertising, they will be less receptive to the recommendation and their recognition of the product involved will be correspondingly lower ([Lee & Shin, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0110)). Previous studies have shown that individuals' judgment and evaluation of information sources will produce different degrees of information acceptance, which may positively or negatively affect the perception, emotions, or opinions of information recipients ([Yuan, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691824000453#bb0250)). Coping refers to an individual's cognitive and behavioral efforts to manage stressful situation ([Lazaru

enriched_points.expanded_content: Advanced algorithms enhance targeted advertising by analyzing user data, ensuring ads reach the right audience, thus improving engagement and conversion rates significantly.
Marketers continuously adapt content strategies based on algorithmic insights, allowing for personalized messages that resonate with specific audience segments and optimize engagement.
Algorithms provide real-time analytics, enabling marketers to measure campaign effectiveness accurately, adjust strategies promptly, and maximize return on investment.
As algorithms prioritize paid content, achieving organic reach becomes increasingly difficult, compelling brands to invest more in advertising to maintain visibility.
